evals.prompt: We sold a $12k annual subscription today. The customer paid upfront. How much revenue do we recognize this month?
evals.expected_output: Should identify this as a multi-period performance obligation. Should calculate the monthly revenue ($1k). Should explain the deferral of the remaining $11k as Unearned Revenue. Should provide a Revenue Recognition Memo structure.
assertions: Calculates monthly revenue as $1,000
Identifies $11,000 as Deferred/Unearned Revenue
Mentions the 5-Step Model (Step 5: Recognize over time)
Provides journal entries for the initial payment and monthly rec
Output follows Revenue Recognition Memo structure

evals.prompt: We sell a 3-year software license for 300k bundled with critical implementation services (we're the only ones who can implement) and 3 years of support (sold standalone at 30k/yr). How many performance obligations and what pattern?
evals.expected_output: License + implementation are NOT distinct (highly interdependent, single integrated PO); support IS distinct. Allocate on SSP (residual or cost-plus for the bundle vs 90k support), recognize the integrated PO over the implementation-to-license period as appropriate and support ratably.
assertions: Combines license and implementation into one PO with the integration rationale
Treats support as a separate PO at observable SSP
Allocates the transaction price on relative SSP
Sets over-time vs point-in-time pattern per PO with justification

evals.prompt: Mid-year, a customer on a 100k/yr 3-year service contract adds a second service line for 60k/yr - our standalone price is 80k/yr. Account for the modification.
evals.expected_output: Added services are distinct but NOT at standalone selling price -> not a separate contract; treat prospectively (terminate-and-replace): remaining consideration (unrecognized original + new) reallocated over remaining goods/services. Show the math.
assertions: Tests both conditions: distinctness AND SSP pricing
Rejects separate-contract treatment because price is below SSP
Applies prospective reallocation over the remaining term
Demonstrates the revised revenue per period numerically

evals.prompt: Contract C-101 in the attached file (evals/files/contracts.csv) was signed on 1 January for a fixed price of $100,000. Support and implementation are both delivered evenly over 12 months. Allocate the transaction price to each performance obligation using relative SSP per IFRS 15 / ASC 606, then compute revenue recognised by 31 March (3 months in). Show the allocation table and journal-ready amounts.
evals.expected_output: Relative-SSP allocation: licence $60,000, support $24,000, implementation $16,000 (sums to $100,000). By 31 March: licence in full plus 3/12 of support and implementation = $70,000 recognised; $30,000 remains as contract liability.
assertions: Allocates approx $60,000 to the software licence
Allocates approx $24,000 to support
Allocates approx $16,000 to implementation
Allocation sums exactly to the $100,000 transaction price
Revenue recognised by 31 March approx $70,000
States the unrecognised balance is a contract liability (deferred revenue)
Computes with code

evals.prompt: [adversarial] Allocate the $100,000 transaction price for contract C-201 in the attached file (evals/files/contract_missing_ssp.csv). Note one obligation has no established SSP.
evals.expected_output: Must recognize the training SSP is missing and apply an estimation method (adjusted market, expected-cost-plus-margin, or residual - residual only if criteria met), state the judgment, and not default to using the stated price as SSP without saying so.
assertions: Identifies that training has no established SSP
Names a legitimate SSP estimation approach (cost-plus-margin, adjusted market, or residual with criteria)
States the estimation as a judgment requiring documentation
Allocation still sums exactly to $100,000
Computes with code
